metadata.title: Use PostgreSQL for Task-Graph Relational Mapping
metadata.author: System Architect

context: We are building a dependency-aware execution engine to manage hierarchical and graph-based relationships between workspace components. We need to select a primary datastore capable of traversing deep dependency trees rapidly while maintaining strong transactional guarantees for state updates during execution.
decision: We will use PostgreSQL 16, leveraging Recursive Common Table Expressions (CTEs) and the `ltree` extension for hierarchical data querying, rather than adopting a dedicated NoSQL Graph Database.

alternativesConsidered.name: Neo4j
pros: First-class support for complex graph traversal
Cypher query language is highly expressive for relationships
cons: Introduces a new infrastructure dependency to maintain
Overkill for the projected depth of our workspace component graphs (rarely > 10 levels deep)
alternativesConsidered.reasonForRejection: The operational overhead of introducing and maintaining a separate graph database cluster outweighs the traversal performance benefits for our specific, relatively shallow tree structures.

alternativesConsidered.name: DynamoDB (Adjacency List Pattern)
alternativesConsidered.description: AWS managed NoSQL key-value store using adjacency lists to represent graphs.
pros: Highly scalable at consistent single-digit millisecond latency
cons: Complex application-side logic required to manage cascading updates
Multi-step recursive queries require multiple network round trips
alternativesConsidered.reasonForRejection: Lacks native recursive query support, which would force the agent execution harness to handle pagination and memory management during complex tree resolutions.

positive: No new infrastructure stack to provision; we can use our existing RDS clusters.
Full ACID compliance ensures component execution states are never left orphaned.
Engineers are already highly proficient with SQL and Postgres tuning.
negative: Extremely dense or highly interconnected cyclic graphs (if introduced later) will suffer performance penalties compared to native graph DBs.
Recursive CTEs can be difficult to read and debug for complex traversals.
neutral: We will need to establish strict schema validation rules early to prevent infinite recursion in CTE queries.
